title: L1 vs L2: The Big Difference
concept: Ridge (L2) squares weights, keeping all features; Lasso (L1) uses absolute values, driving weights to zero.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why is Lasso Regression (L1) useful for simplifying models?

options.text: Because it zeroes out useless weights, acting as an automatic feature selector
Because it doubles the number of training samples
Because it automatically plots a decision boundary for you

exercises.explain: Right! By driving useless weights to exactly zero, Lasso performs automatic feature selection.

cards.title: The main event
cards.body: Ridge (L2) and Lasso (L1) are the two titans of regularization. They both fight overfitting, but their methods are completely different. Choosing the right one determines your model's final shape.

cards.title: L1 vs L2 penalties
cards.body: The core difference lies in the math: L2 (Ridge) penalizes squared weights (w²), while L1 (Lasso) penalizes absolute weights (|w|). This tiny algebraic difference changes how weights shrink.

cards.title: Leaky pipe vs. drain
cards.body: Think of L2 (Ridge) like cooling down hot soup: everything shrinks slightly, but all the ingredients remain in the bowl. Think of L1 (Lasso) like a colander: it drains out the water (less important features) completely.

cards.title: Feature preservation
cards.body: If you have 10 features, Ridge might change the weights to [0.1, 0.2, 0.05...] (all non-zero). Lasso might change them to [0.3, 0.0, 0.0, 0.5...], leaving only 3 active features.

cards.title: L1 isn't always superior
cards.body: A common myth is that Lasso (L1) is always better because it selects features. But if you have many small, useful features (like in gene sequencing), Ridge (L2) performs better because it doesn't throw anything away.

cards.title: Comparing L1 and L2
cards.body: Ridge vs. Lasso side by side:

left.text:
Penalizes squared weights.
Forces weights close to 0.
Keeps all features.
Best for dense data.

right.text:
Penalizes absolute weights.
Forces weights to exactly 0.
Creates sparse models.
Best for sparse data.

exercises.prompt: What mathematical operation differentiates L2 (Ridge) from L1 (Lasso) regularization?

options.text: L2 squares the weights, while L1 takes their absolute values
L2 multiplies weights, while L1 divides them
L2 takes the square root, while L1 cubes the weights

exercises.explain: Ridge squares the weights (L2), whereas Lasso uses absolute values (L1).

exercises.prompt: True or false: Ridge regression (L2) can completely remove features by setting their weights to zero.
exercises.explain: False! Ridge shrinks weights close to zero, but mathematically it never sets them to exactly zero.

exercises.sentence: If you have a dataset where every single feature is slightly useful, you should prefer ___ regression.
exercises.explain: Ridge keeps all features, which is ideal when all features have a small but real effect.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following outputs represents a Lasso (L1) model's weights?
exercises.explain: Lasso creates sparsity, meaning many weights are exactly 0.0.

exercises.prompt: You are predicting customer satisfaction using 10 features. You know that all 10 features (like speed, quality, and price) have a small, positive impact on satisfaction. You want to avoid overfitting. Which should you choose?

options.text: Ridge regression (L2), because it retains all features while shrinking their weights.
Lasso regression (L1), because it will delete half of the features.
No regularization, to make sure the weights stay as large as possible.

exercises.explain: Spot on! When every feature has a small impact, you don't want to delete any. Ridge shrinks them all safely. Next up: ElasticNet!
